petitioner_background: The petitioner is a competitive martial arts fighter from Armenia who practices a specific style of karate and has competed in local, national, and international sparring (kumite) competitions since 1999, predominantly in Armenia. He received a black belt certificate in 2005 at age 16 and seeks classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in athletics under EB-1A to continue training and competing in karate competitions in the United States. The petition was filed on August 21, 2017.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) First place certificate and trophy from the 2013 Open Championship in the men's division (with photos on winner's podium); (2) First place certificate from a 2017 competition; (3) Second place certificate in kumite in adult category at the 8th European Championships (2016); (4) First place certificate at a Cup event (2014); (5) First and second place certificates in kumite adult category at Championship (2014, 2013, 2012, 2010, 2007); (6) First place certificate at Eastern Martial Arts Federation Armenia Open Tournament (2013); (7) First place certificate at Armenian National Association Open Championship (2013); (8) First place certificates at Armenian Open Championship (2012, 2011); (9) First, second, and third place certificates at Armenian National Championships (2012, 2011, 2010, 2008, 2007); (10) Second place certificate in kumite adult category at Championship (2009); (11) First place finish in kumite adult category at 10th Open championship (2009); (12) Second place certificate at World Championship (2008); (13) Youth competition podium finishes between 1999-2006; (14) Black belt certificate from 2005; (15) Letters from the president of the federation stating petitioner was chosen in 2005 by the Armenian Karate Federation (AKF) to be a member of the Armenian national team after achieving a podium finish in the championship.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the documentary evidence did not demonstrate that the petitioner's first, second, and third place certificates were recognized at a level commensurate with nationally or internationally recognized awards for excellence in the field. The petitioner did not submit supporting evidence such as official entry requirements from sponsoring organizations or other evidence to establish the significance of the events within the sport. A karate competition being open to athletes from throughout a country is not adequate alone to establish national/international recognition. The black belt certificate reflects a rank promotion based on a skills test, not a prize or award, and lacks media reports demonstrating national/international recognition. However, the AAO found the petitioner's podium finish in the championship, which resulted in his appointment to the Armenian national team, sufficient to qualify as a nationally recognized award for excellence in the field. The Director had not addressed this evidence. Therefore, the petitioner met this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner claimed membership in the World Karate Federation (WKF), another federation, and the Armenian national team. He submitted two letters from the president of the federation stating that in 2005 the petitioner was chosen by the Armenian Karate Federation (AKF), the national governing body of the sport, to be a member of the Armenian national team after achieving a podium finish in the championship, undergoing specialized training, and winning the finals round of competition in his age and weight group.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that to meet this criterion, a petitioner must show that the association requires outstanding achievement as an essential condition for admission. The record did not include evidence of the membership requirements for the WKF or the other federation, such as bylaws or rules of admission showing those organizations require outstanding achievements of their members as judged by recognized national or international experts. However, regarding the Armenian national team, the AAO found the petitioner met this criterion based on his selection for Armenia's national team, which required outstanding achievement (podium finish in championship, specialized training, and winning finals in his age/weight group). The Director had not addressed this evidence.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) A 2017 interview on a sports program on PanArmenian TV, a U.S.-based Armenian television channel, with a translated transcript about him and his career achievements, plus a media kit indicating the channel is available on most major Southern California Cable Television providers; (2) A 2006 article in the Armenian National Agrarian University (ANAU) newspaper; (3) A translated article asserted to be published on verdadzortert.com, with a letter from the editor of Vardadzor indicating it is a regional newspaper with print circulation of 10,000-15,000 readers per month; (4) A 2018 article on yerkirmedia.am; (5) Translated excerpts of an interview for Armenia First Television Italy and an article from 'Germany Bremen.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found multiple deficiencies: (1) The PanArmenian TV interview did not demonstrate who interviewed the petitioner or the date of broadcast, and the petitioner did not provide viewership statistics for the specific program; (2) The 2006 ANAU article was not about the petitioner but about the Yerevan Martial Arts Club, and did not identify the author; (3) Neither the translation nor the translator's certification identified the source where the verdadzortert.com article was published, and the editor's letter did not show that Vardadzor's website visitor numbers elevated it to major media; (4) The 2018 yerkirmedia.am article was published after the August 21, 2017 filing date, and petitioner must establish eligibility from time of filing through adjudication per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1); (5) The Armenia First Television Italy and 'Germany Bremen' materials could not be considered 'published material' since petitioner did not submit the original source documents. The petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted letters of support from personal contacts discussing his achievements as a karate competitor, including: (1) Two letters from the federation president stating petitioner's first place finishes at the 2013 event in Italy and 2017 event conferred national and international recognition for his achievements; (2) Letters from martial arts instructors in the United States who met the petitioner at the 2017 competition, describing him as very talented and hardworking with exceptional fighting skills; (3) A letter from a martial arts instructor who offered the petitioner employment in the U.S. as a competitive karate athlete, mentioning that in 2017 the petitioner trained at his facility and was featured in the PanArmenia TV interview; (4) A letter describing and praising the petitioner's 'very unique and difficult' sparring technique and highlighting his second place finish at the 2008 event.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the letters primarily contained attestations of the petitioner's status in the field without providing specific examples of contributions rising to a level consistent with major significance. Letters that specifically articulate how a petitioner's contributions are of major significance and their impact on subsequent work add value; letters that lack specifics and use hyperbolic language do not and are not probative. USCIS need not accept primarily conclusory statements. While the petitioner competed in tournaments and participated in extensive training, he did not show how these activities equate to 'original' athletic contributions of major significance. The contributions must be not only original but of major significance. The evidence showed he was knowledgeable and skilled but did not demonstrate his impact on the sport was commensurate with an original athletic contribution of major significance. The petitioner did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner asserted he performed in a leading or critical role for the Armenian national team in his role as an athlete on the team in 2005, claiming that all team members 'must possess and exhibit special skills and achievements, which set them apart from their peers.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO explained that a leading role is evidenced from the role itself, and a critical role is one in which the petitioner contributed in a way of significant importance to the outcome of the organization's activities. The petitioner did not submit evidence to support his assertions, such as team rosters, team statistics, or other documentation of his tenure with the Armenian national team. The evidence did not elucidate how the petitioner's position differentiated him from other karate athletes on the team, such as its senior players. The federation president's letters primarily attested to the petitioner's work ethic and sportsmanship but did not suggest he performed in a leading or critical role. The evidence did not establish he was responsible for the team's success or standing consistent with 'leading or critical role.' Additionally, the record did not contain sufficient evidence to demonstrate that the Armenian national team has a distinguished reputation in the field. The petitioner did not meet this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner did not submit the required initial evidence of either a one-time achievement or documents meeting at least three of the ten regulatory criteria. Although the AAO found the petitioner met two criteria (awards and membership) based on his selection for the Armenian national team, he failed to meet a third criterion. The AAO thus did not need to fully address a final merits determination, but advised that it had reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding that the petitioner established the level of expertise required. The AAO noted that even athletes performing at the major league level do not automatically meet the extraordinary ability standard (citing Matter of Price), and the petitioner had not shown his achievements were indicative of sustained national or international acclaim or consistent with a 'career of acclaimed work in the field' as contemplated by Congress.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r. 1994)
Matter of Skirball Cultural Ctr., 25 I&N Dec. 799 (AAO 2012)
1756, Inc. v. The U.S. Att'y Gen., 745 F. Supp. 9 (D.C. Dist. 1990)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: No fraud findings. Procedural note: The AAO found that the Director had failed to address evidence regarding the petitioner's selection for the Armenian national team, which the AAO found sufficient to meet two criteria (awards and membership). Additionally, the AAO noted that certain evidence (a 2018 yerkirmedia.am article and 2018 awards received in California) was obtained after the August 21, 2017 filing date and could not be considered, as the petitioner must establish eligibility from the time of filing through adjudication per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1).
